Looking at ACWV’s sectoral split, we see that the portfolio is aimed at stability with a 13.40% stake in Communication helped by leading telecom companies that provide steady cash flows across all markets or the 10.47% stake in Consumer Staples that enhance the defensive aspect of the portfolio and the growing demand for essentials in emerging markets. More aggressive, and given the current economic environment, the source of lagging performance vs. the USMV, the 21.39% allocation to Information Technology companies. The allocation to Financials, Healthcare and Utilities complement the defensive aspect of the portfolio and cyclical exposure is added through Energy and Real Estate (even if I could argue that energy is now defensive).

USMV, has a heavier allocation to IT, almost 30% of the fund and reflects on the leading position of the U.S. in that industry. I believe this is the source of the outperformance against ACWV.

My actionable idea here is to get ACWV for international exposure while taking into account the lack of IT companies that you can complement with a sector specific ETF, it doesn’t have to be a minimum volatility one, the goal is to slightly increase the exposure to U.S. tech if you want to be more aggressive.

Financial Metrics

Global diversification often comes with a premium and ACWV makes no exception with an expense ratio of 0.20% against 0.15% for USMV. This is not a big difference. Looking at both funds’ AUM, it makes sense to see that the size of USMV is larger, $23.4 Bn against $3.3 Bn for ACWV. Both funds are benefiting from a strong investor appetite but the U.S. focus has been the winning strategy over the last 5 years so investors naturally benefit from USMV. USMV is also more liquid, with a daily trading volume of $197.9 Mn (0.8% of AUM), proportionally twice as high as that of ACWV: $14.2 Mn (0.4% of AUM).

However some factors are pointing in the direction of ACWV: the fund’s dividend yield is higher: 2.36% (backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 2.03%) against 1.58% for USMV (backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 1.45%). Also, valuation metrics are favoring ACWV with a P/E ratio of 21 and a P/B ratio of 2.75 against 25.92 and 4.23 for USMV. ACWV is a lot cheaper.

Now, I mentioned them briefly before, let’s look at performances:

In the short term, reflecting on the current tense market conditions, USMV delivered higher returns, but in the medium to long term, USMV is outperforming. The dynamic between ACWV and USMV performance is driven by market conditions, basically the more uncertain the market is, the more I see ACWV outperforming.

Risks

Both ETFs are facing different risks, but given they both are significantly exposed to the U.S., even ACWV it means that they are highly dependent on the economic situation there. I view the tariff situation as a risk, as well as the volatility of interest rates, marked with a lot of speculation on when the next rate cut, and at what point we will end the year.

Sectoral concentration also is a risk, both funds are heavily invested in tech, a sector with high valuations and thus a high level of volatility. Thankfully the minimum volatility strategy alleviates that risk, but both funds remain tied to the performance of the sector.

For ACWV, given 40% of the fund is invested outside of the U.S. with different currencies, the fund’s performance is dependent on the performance of the dollar index. This is particularly a risk in emerging markets with volatile currencies, but this is a risk you can hedge.

Liquidity risks are not very important, but I think it’s still relevant to note that if it is a concern to you, USMV is twice as liquid as ACWV.

Conclusion

Both ACWV and USMV are solid options to access minimum volatility investing. It is hard to draw a clear conclusion on which one is the best but, I do believe that if you are looking into minimum volatility funds to increase the defensive part of your portfolio, you should go with ACWV. The medium to long term underperformance of the fund is mainly explained by a lesser exposure to tech (that you can fix yourself if needed). And global diversification adds to the defensive aspect.

However if you want a more aggressive fund you should go with USMV and its focus on the US and higher valuation metrics.

I see two tactics here: you can start buying USMV and wait to see if the economic situation deteriorates or not in the coming months and then switch to ACWV that will be more defensive, or you can directly buy ACWV and adjust your tech holdings to match that of USMV. Personally, I like the second option better because it means that in the future you will just have to manage your tech exposure as part of your portfolio, so it doesn’t really change much for you.

data.title: CSG Systems: A 'Hold' After The Recent Run
data.description:
Introduction

CSG Systems (NASDAQ:CSGS) is a SaaS platform company with a specific focus on revenue management and business support systems. The majority of its customers are in the telecommunications industry and until recently its two largest customers (Charter and Comcast) represented almost half of the revenue.

Fortunately CSG Systems has started to actively diversify away from being too exposed to just two customers.

A robust performance in the first semester resulted in a guidance hike

Compared to the second quarter of last year, the total revenue in the most recent quarter increased by approximately 2.5%. Interestingly, the cost of revenue decreased by just over 1% and this of course resulted in a rather noticeable increase of the gross profit. As the income statement below also shows, although the other operating expenses increased by approximately 2% as well, the reported operating income jumped by approximately 18% to just under $30 million.

The company's net finance expense also remains under control thanks to a decent but decreasing interest income and after deducting the $3.6 million in other expenses, CSG Systems reported a pre-tax income of just under $20 million, resulting in a bottom-line result of $12.3 million in net profit. This represents an EPS of $0.44. And looking at the company's performance in the first half of the year, we see a net profit of 28.4 million which resulted in an EPS of $1.02 per share. This includes approximately $5.8 million in ‘other expenses’ and it also includes approximately $12 million in restructuring expenses.

While the financial results don't look very inspiring based on the reported numbers, there are quite a few elements that are a drag on those reported results. Fortunately the company also provides a breakdown of the non-GAAP results, shown below. While I'm usually quite reluctant to use non-GAAP numbers, it is interesting to see that for instance in the first half of the year, there are quite a few elements that weighed on the results but either are a non cash expense or are related to acquisitions.

As you can see below, the non-GAAP net income in the first half of the year was just under $65 million.

As the table above also shows, this would have represented an EPS of $2.29 per share which is an increase of 13% compared to the first semester of 2024.

The impact of those non-cash elements is also clearly visible in the cash flow statement. As you can see below, CSG Systems reported an operating cash flow of 48.8 million and after taking the change in the working capital position into consideration as well as the lease payments, the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately $78.8M.

The total capex was approximately $7.2 million, resulting in an underlying net free cash flow of approximately $71.5M or roughly $2.48 per share.

A robust result, and the majority of the free cash flow was spent on dividends and share buybacks, in line with the company’s policy. As you can see below, the company aims to distribute at least $100 million to its shareholders. We can assume the dividends will require approximately $35 to $36 million this year which means there should be a budget to buy back at least $65 million of stock which will help to keep the total amount of shares outstanding at the reasonable level given the relatively high amount of stock based compensation.

Thanks to the robust performance in the second quarter and the first half of this year, the company has increased its guidance for the entire financial year. Looking at the non-GAAP guidance for this year, the company has slightly hiked its EBITDA and free cash flow guidance.

CSG Systems now aims to generate a non-GAAP EPS of $4.65-4.90 per share, while the adjusted free cash flow should come in between $120 million and $150 million. Using the midpoint of that guidance ($135M) and assuming an average share count of 29 million shares, the free cash flow per share will come in at $4.65.

Investment thesis

While the financial results are quite robust I also appreciate the company's push to diversify away from its two main customers (Charter and Comcast). While these two clients represented approximately 40% of CSG’s revenue in Q2 2024, this was reduced to approximately 36% in the second quarter of the current financial year.

At the end of June, CSG Systems had a net debt position of approximately $426 million (and this includes the 35 million in customer deposits as a financial liability). CSG uses a net financial debt of $404M, representing a debt ratio of just around 1.5 times the adjusted EBITDA, which is quite reasonable.

CSG Systems is trading at pretty much the same earnings multiple compared to when my previous article was published. I’m still a ‘hold’ on CSG Systems but I could potentially be interested on any weakness. I definitely missed the opportunity earlier this year when the stock was trading at just around $50/share.

data.title: Brown-Forman: Sobering Headwinds, Intoxicating Value
data.description:
Introduction

Brown-Forman (NYSE: NYSE:BF.A, NYSE:BF.B) has been a staple of bars and parties for decades, with its vast portfolio of iconic brands that stemmed from highly recognizable whiskeys. Its offerings include strong brands such as the highly popular whiskey brand Jack Daniel’s, but also Woodford Reserve and Herradura. Though it is clear that the spirit industry is facing some headwinds in which I will delve into later on in this analysis.

I believe that Brown-Forman might be a strong opportunity for a turnaround, and with its recent fiscal Q1 earnings, now is the right time for a fresh analysis on the company.

Q1 Earnings and Current Dynamics

Brown-Forman just announced relatively mixed earnings with a revenue of $924MM, down 2.8% Y/Y, while also posting a deceleration of 13% towards its EPS at 36 cents, narrowly missing the consensus of 37 cents. BF also reported a decline in its gross profit to $552MM by over 2%. The main reason has been the drop in sales in the flagship Jack Daniel’s whisky in the US, even with successful international launches of derived products. The Tequila segment has also declined due to a drop in Herradura sales, though El Jimador showed promise. The Ready-to-Drink segment has been a bright spot, with sales increasing by 6% thanks to a 26% surge in New Mix sales in Mexico. The rest of the portfolio saw significant declines, but that was mainly due to the divestment of Finlandia brands and Sonoma-Cutrer.

Geographically, Brown-Forman has seen poor form in both the US and developed markets due to soft consumer demand, especially with the geopolitical and economic uncertainties. Though emerging markets have delivered strong growth, with net sales increasing by a massive 20% thanks mainly to Brazil and Turkey.

The company has reaffirmed its guidance for its full fiscal year 2026 with the expectation that its organic net sales and operating income to experience a decline in the low single-digit range. This tells us that management is pragmatic about the current condition of the market with consumer uncertainty and potential tariffs.

I believe that the strategy of Brown-Forman of banking on flavor innovation and targeted marketing will support the brand going forward, though near term, the premium spirits market is looking disappointing. The company also made some entries in the gin and rum categories, which could offer more demand than whisky in this current market. Though I believe that these avenues will contribute to a relatively small revenue share in the near future.

The main headwind has been rising consumer caution regarding the impact that alcohol has on health. I’ve noticed the behavioral change to be honest with many of my friends, reducing their alcohol intake or even going off alcohol, while the younger generation has even been more drastic. There is also a trend of low-alcohol beverages that have been experiencing strong growth with craft mocktails and alcohol-free beers. Other external factors that have also amplified this trend include the rise of GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic, which has reduced the appetite for alcohol for many consumers, as well as cannabis use, which has been competing for the same recreational customers.

Valuation

Establishing an undervaluation thesis for BF is a tricky endeavor, given the headwinds that the company has. Brown-Forman has a current P/E Non-GAAP of 16.55, which is in line with its peers, while also having an EV/EBITDA in line with the sector median at 13.52. The company has a FCF yield of 4.25% as well and has been relatively strong at generating FCF over the past 10 years. Though its FCF yield has mainly increased based on the downturn in share prices. Outside of financial metrics, the company’s value comes from intangible assets, including brand prowess and further entrenched client partners in its whiskey business. Its iconic, 150-year-old, whisky brand Jack Daniel’s has been a bestseller forever, and with its other bourbon brands, holds the largest market share of the American Whisky segment. Another moat could be the significant amount of capital tied in warehousing and again inventories, which in the case of BF, have over a three-year maturation process. The company has over $1.6B worth of aging inventories.

Growth is the main culprit of the company losing value over the past couple of years, though if I focus on the positives, BF has done an exceptional job at rolling out good derived products with strong pricing ladders under the Jack Daniel’s umbrella. Five new whiskies have been launched over the past decade, such as honey and cinnamon flavors that have posted strong sales, as well as RTD beverages that offer both convenience and a lower calorie/alcohol content alternative. Regarding the ultra-premium spectrum, BF has done a strong job at focusing on bottles that can exceed $1,000, which aligns with the premiumization trend in overall alcohol consumption. This is mainly due to the “drink less but better” motto of current alcohol consumers.

Regarding profitability, BF has some strong overall metrics with great margins, such as the peer-leading 21.86% net income margin. Its gross margin is in line with its peers at 58.94%. The focus on brands such as Woodford Reserve and the higher-end expressions of Jack Daniel’s should continue to support these high margins as well as the company’s pricing power. BF also excels regarding its return metrics thanks to the high-quality of its business and the recognition of its brands that don’t require as many marketing resources.

Risks

Outside of the headwinds that I mentioned, spirits makers are also exposed to regulatory headwinds that could increase taxes on their products, thus rendering them more expensive for consumers and reducing demand. Further health warning labels could also discourage consumption or even tarnish the brands.

Brown-Forman is also facing increasing competition from craft distillers that offer an attractive alternative to the established brands that have been there forever. Though the entrenched position of Jack Daniel’s in the American Whisky industry certainly provides protection, thanks to the brand recognition and the capital requirements of the industry.

Conclusion

Brown-Forman has probably the best portfolio of alcohol products among its peers and that's why I believe that it will not be as affected by the current headwinds of the industry. It has great profitability metrics and with its intangible value, I believe that we can make the case of a relative undervaluation. I rate the company as a buy.

data.title: American Express: Risks To Growth Are Not Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
American Express (NYSE:AXP) has been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 26%. Its strong growth among millennials and focus on higher-income consumers have helped to power results and minimized exposure to some credit deterioration among lower income households. I last covered shares of American Express (AXP) in March, rating the stock a “hold.” With shares up 25% since then, a “buy” would have been merited, though I did suggest buying shares on pullbacks to $240, a level shares reached in April before bouncing back strongly. As shares are at 52-weeks high and some concerns over discretionary spending (particularly in travel & leisure) have mounted, now is a good time to reevaluate AXP. I would take profits.

On August 15th, American Express reported July consumer credit quality data with a US delinquency rate of 1.3%, flat at that level for a third straight month. Charge-offs remain in keeping with recent trends, at 2.0%. Consumer loans were up 1.2% sequentially to $93.7 billion, 9.1% higher than a year ago, as consumers continue to spend but also rebuild balances at a higher pace than their level of spending. Essentially though, this monthly update confirmed that there has been no major change in what the company is seeing in terms of credit trends, relative to the second quarter.

Of course, American Express’s customer base tends to skew higher income, given its focus on premium cards. Higher income consumers are generally more resilient during periods of economic volatility, given more stable employment and higher savings level. That has played out this cycle. As you can see below, AXP’s delinquency rates across age cohorts are substantially better than the industry norm. Current delinquency rates are comparable to pre-COVID levels (a healthy area), and they have been broadly stable.

Given my view that the US is unlikely to enter a recession (though growth will likely stay muted), I do not expect delinquencies to reaccelerate from current levels, instead remaining broadly stable. Because higher-end consumers tend to spend more and be more resilient during periods of economic volatility, they are highly sought after. If anything, competition for these customers is likely to heat up.

Notably, Citigroup (C) is launching a new premium credit card to better compete with AXP and JPMorgan (JPM), who dominate the niche. I do not expect this offering to be a game changer for growth, but it is an incremental challenge and at the least may increase the cost of acquiring and maintaining its customers. I also continue to be impressed by where AXP is generating growth.

Over 60% of new cards are going to millennials who focus more of their spending on experiences than goods, in keeping with AXP’s traditional area of strength. These customers are especially valuable as AXP may be acquiring a customer it can keep for decades. The degree to which AXP has built brand relevance among younger consumers while maintaining its core customer base has been exceedingly impressive. These consumers have been also growing spending at a faster clip than older consumers, which has been supportive for AXP’s overall volume growth rate.

Still, there was a bit of a deceleration in growth last quarter. AXP won 3.1 million new accounts, down about 6% from last year, though most of the weakness was overseas. One quarter does not make a trend, but I view slowing card growth as potential risk that bears close monitoring, especially given incremental competition.

Aside from increased competition, this has come against a backdrop of moderating spending, particularly in travel. This is a key exposure for American Express as T&E (travel and entertainment) is 27% of its business. As you can see below, revenue per available room has been consistently negative this summer, primarily due to lower occupancy, as rates have been either side of flat. This is consistent with other data, such as lower air travel to Las Vegas. If this persists, that could pressure volumes on AXP’s network.

While these pose risk to growth in the second half of this year, recent results have continued to be solid. I would also be clear that I see competition and T&E exposure as headwinds to the pace of growth rather than likely to cause an outright decline in results. In the company’s second quarter reported on July 18th, American Express earned $4.08, which beat expectations by $0.20 as revenue grew by 9% to $17.9 billion. EPS was up 17% from a year ago, excluding a one-time gain that boosted 2024 net income.

There was broad based strength with cardholders spending 7% more than last year, though we did see T&E tick down to 5%. I view business T&E spending as being especially vulnerable as this is a relatively easy way for businesses to cut spending in the face of increased uncertainty and costs from tariffs. Indeed, small and medium-sized businesses accounts for 82% of its commercial business. Their spending is up 2% with large businesses up 4%. T&E growth is down to just 1% among businesses.

Discount fees were up 6% to $9.4 billion given increased volumes, and I see this revenue stream being particularly vulnerable to a slowdown in T&E spending. Thanks to price increases and faster growth in premium offerings, net card fees rose by 20% to $2.5 billion. I do expect this to slow in 2026, given more competition.

Net interest income was up 12% to $4.2 billion. About half of this growth was due to increased balances and half due to a wider margin as credit card rates do not fall as quickly as the fed funds rate, making AXP a beneficiary of the rate cutting cycle we have seen thus far. AXP took $1.4 billion of provisions in Q2, up slightly from a year ago, even as its 2.0% net charge-offs were down 10bps from a year ago. It built $222 million in reserves, largely reflecting loan balance growth. Reserves are 2.9% of loans, a healthy level, and I expect just modest ongoing reserve growth.

Now, we are also seeing pressure on expenses given both its growth and increased rewards to maintain the attractiveness of its offerings. Rewards were up 9% to $4.6 billion. Marketing was up a modest 5% to $1.55 billion. This slower marketing growth may be a reason we have seen card additions slow, especially with new product launches from peers. I view expense risk as skewed to the upside given industry trends.

Importantly, AXP maintains a strong balance sheet with a 10.6% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio, right in line with its 10-11% target. As such, it continues to return capital to investors. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 2.5% from last year. It also has a 1% dividend yield. Given the need to retain some capital to support ongoing business and balance sheet growth, I expect capital returns to remain in the 3-3.5% area. Of course, if growth did slow more materially, AXP could return a higher share of earnings to investors.

Alongside results, AXP reiterated guidance for $15-$15.50 in EPS on 8-10% revenue growth. Back in March, I was targeting $15 in 2025 EPS. With the dollar being a bit weaker, boosting international results when translated back into USD, I now see AXP on track for ~$15.25 in earnings this year. Assuming 2 rate cuts this year and stable provisions, I expect AXP to generate about $16-$16.50 in earnings over the next twelve months with lower T&E spending posing some downside risk.

That leaves shares at 20x earnings, which is very expensive for a financial company. While AXP has historically traded at a premium valuation given its strong brand and unique card network, AXP is also trading now at a premium to itself in addition to a massive premium to peers for an “F” valuation according to Seeking Alpha’s screen.

I struggle to support a 20x valuation, especially when I see all risks skewed to growth decelerating from here rather than accelerating. AXP is well-cushioned against a rise in delinquencies, but competition is rising and T&E faces headwinds. With a capital return yield down below 3.5%, I do not see any margin for error and am cautious that slowing growth could rerate shares lower. Even returning to its own 5-year average multiple would push shares below $290. With growth slowing, one could even argue its multiple should be lower than its 5-year average.

I am always cautious rating a very high-quality company a “sell,” but sometimes valuation is just too high. With so much good news already in the price and downside risks to growth largely ignored, I struggle to see AXP continuing to outperform. Even a full but fair valuation points to a 10% downside. As such, I view this as an attractive time to take profits and am moving to “sell.” I would want shares slower to $275-290 to consider re-entering AXP.

data.title: Is Alphabet A Buy As It Retests The All Time Peak?
data.description:
GOOG Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Rally - A Dip Buying Opportunity May Be Coming

We previously covered Alphabet Inc., aka Google (GOOG, GOOGL, GOOG:CA) in July 2025, discussing how its discounted valuation at FWD P/E of 18x was mind-blowing then, despite the increasingly dominant positions in AI, streaming, cloud, and robo-taxi, amongst others.

Combined with Gemini reporting accelerating user growth compared to OpenAI's ChatGPT, the highly profitable YouTube/Google Cloud segments, and the new growth opportunities arising from Waymo's +150% YoY growth in paid rides, it was apparent that the well-diversified Big Tech company was likely to outperform in the long term.

GOOG 1Y Stock Price

Since that Buy rating, GOOG has further rallied by +18% well outperforming the wider market at +3.5%, with a similar outperformance also observed in its Big Tech/Magnificent 7 peers in varying degrees.

The exuberance is unsurprising indeed, since the Q2'25 earning season has brought forth excellent clarity surrounding the durability of the AI boom and the robust advertising spending trends, as reiterated by numerous market leaders across the infrastructure, SaaS, social media, and ad-tech layers.

With the pronounced relief after the tariff-induced meltdown in April 2025 already triggering massive market-wide rallies by the time of writing, it is unsurprising that FOMO traders/investors have continued to feed the ongoing rally post-earnings.

This is especially since GOOG's Google Cloud has reported expanding global cloud market share to 13% by Q2'25 (+1 points QoQ/+1 YoY), at the expense of two of its direct competitors, including Microsoft Azure's (MSFT) ongoing erosion to 20% (-2 points QoQ/-3 YoY) and Amazon's AWS (AMZN) to 30% (+1 points QoQ/-2 YoY).

The same robust numbers have been observed in Google Cloud's expanding revenues to $13.62B (+11% QoQ/+31.7% YoY) and richer operating margins of 20.7% (+3.1 points QoQ/+9.4 YoY/+72.7 from FY2019 levels of -52%), as its multi-year backlog also grows to $108.2B (+17% QoQ/+37.3% YoY), with it underscoring the excellent cross-selling/adoption trends and the improving operational momentum thus far.

These reasons are also why it makes sense for GOOG to raise their FY2025 capex guidance to $85B (+61.8% YoY), up from the prior guidance of $75B (+42.7% YoY) offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, while hinting at further capex increase in FY2026:

While we have been working hard to increase capacity and have improved the pace of server deployment, we expect to remain in a tight demand supply environment going into 2026. (Seeking Alpha)

These underscore why GOOG remains a great force to be reckoned with, significantly aided by Gemini 2.5 Pro finally meeting OpenAI's GPT-4o head to head in the ongoing AI race by June 2025, while still holding up against OpenAI's GPT-5 (recently released in August 2025).

The AI benefit has been observed in GOOG's improved advertising performance as well, as similarly reported by Meta (META), with their improved AI/agentic capabilities already delivering improved ad targeting, growing user engagement, and expanded monetization cadence.

This is on top of the intensified integration across its existing search verticals/commerce/video capabilities, the reinventing of its ad-purchase platform, and the accretive impact from its AI-powered asset generation tools, allowing GOOG to drive higher ad volumes and Return On Ad Spending [ROAS], which in turn drives its ad-related growth flywheel thus far.

These developments already triggered GOOG's higher advertising revenues of $71.34B (+6.6% QOQ/+10.4% YoY) and the Google Services' still rich operating margins at 40% (-2.2 points QoQ/-0.1 YoY/+7.8 from FY2019 levels of 32.2%), with it underscoring why advertising has been and will likely remain the company's top and bottom-line driver ahead.

Combined with YouTube's growing share at 13.4% in July 2025 (+0.6 points QoQ/+2.8 YoY) during the secular streaming growth, we can understand why the market has awarded the giant's bright prospects with an outsized +43.1% rally since the April 2025 bottom, as the market grows increasingly certain about its ability to capitalize and monetize the ongoing AI/advertising boom.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

While GOOG has yet to provide any forward guidance, the promising FQ2'25 performance and the management's optimistic commentaries may have led to the consensus raising their forward estimates, with the company expected to report accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +11.6%/+14.2% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +10.5%/+12.8%, while building upon the 5Y historical growth profile at +16.7%/+25.1% and the 10Y growth at +18.2%/+20.2%, respectively.

GOOG Valuations

Despite the upgraded consensus estimates, the ongoing stock price recovery, and the ongoing upgrading from the April 2025 P/E bottom of 16.15x, it goes without saying that GOOG remains extremely cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 20.83x, compared to the 5Y mean of 24.31x and the 10Y mean of 23.76x.

Based on GOOG's FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.40x, we believe that it remains attractively valued compared to the sector median of 1.49x and its Magnificent 7 peers, including:

  • Apple (AAPL) at 2.98x,
  • MSFT at 2.85x,
  • META at 1.55x,
  • AMZN at 1.93x,
  • Nvidia (NVDA) at 1.38x, and
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 10.15x.

These developments demonstrate why GOOG remains a compelling AI investment thesis, thanks to the convincing monetization cadence and the cheap valuations, with it offering interested investors an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is GOOG Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GOOG 5Y Stock Price

These reasons may also be why GOOG has been well supported during its ongoing rally since the November 2022 and January 2023 bottom, with the two established trend lines offering interested investors great insights into its future trading pattern.

Based on the LTM adj EPS of $9.39 (+34.7% sequentially) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 20.83x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to our base-case fair-value estimates of $195.50.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $11.98, we believe that there remains an excellent upside potential of +20.4% to our base-case long-term price target of $249.50 as well.

Given the undeniably discounted valuations, we may also look forward to an even richer upside potential of +79.2% to our bull-case long-term price target of $371.30, assuming an upward re-rating closer to its Mag 7 peer group mean of 31x (aside from TSLA).

GOOG's Overbought Position

On the other hand, our bullish conjecture has been marred by GOOG's notably overbought position by the time of writing, with technical indicators and historical trends implying a potential reversal in momentum as buying power is exhausted after the massive market-wide rally since the April 2025 bottom.

This cadence is not surprising indeed, with a similar development also observed in a few other stocks, which have boasted double/triple digit rallies over the past few months, and a correction seemingly in the cards after the GOOG stock also breaks out from its all-time January 2025 peaks.

This is especially since GOOG is already retesting its secondary resistance levels of $207s, with the stock likely to lose steam here and then return part of its recent gains to bounce off the next support levels/50-day moving averages of $188s in the near-term, before hitting new peaks afterwards.

As a result of the potential -9% correction from current heights, our reiterated Strong Buy rating comes with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer and only adding nearer to the $180s/$190s for an improved long-term return profile.

Patience in the meantime.

data.title: Invivyd: Superior COVID Prophylaxis; Eventual Winner Regardless Of Trump Admin Anti-Vaccine Actions
data.description:
Intro

Over the past couple weeks, Invivyd ($IVVD), the leading and essentially only company advancing anti-COVID monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) as an alternative to COVID vaccines, looked to be entering a reset/rebuilding period after:

  1. Reporting another non-growth quarter for Pemgarda two weeks ago (driven by low COVID case count, burdensome IV administration, and a narrow EUA instead of full approval), emphasizing the importance of advancing its superior, intramuscularly administered VYD2311. This essentially marked defeat on the company’s lofty previous goal of reaching breakeven with Pemgarda sales by the end of 1H25 (which would’ve required ~$26 million of sales vs. $12 million reported).

  2. Announcement of alignment with the FDA on running a single 12-week pivotal trial for the potential approval of VYD2311 in COVID prophylaxis, providing a clear, rapid pathway to approval for its highly potent, long-half life, best-in-class mAb.

  3. The closing of a $57 million (including exercised underwriter’s option) equity and pre-funded warrant financing at $0.52 led by RA Capital and Janus Henderson, essentially doubling the company’s shares outstanding but giving it a clear path to completing its pivotal trial and hopefully launching and reaching profitability with VYD2311, as well as pursuing its early-stage efforts in Long COVID, measles, and RSV.

While this wasn’t the plan back in March when we first started talking about the company, given the circumstances (e.g. Pemgarda not really panning out and the FDA not promoting it from EUA to full approval or expanding its indication as hoped), the new clearly-defined and expedited pathway to VYD2311 approval and fresh, hopefully breakeven-reaching capital from well-regarded investors appeared to be a solid reset point for the company to start building real momentum over the next 6-12 months.

But after Newsweek reported on Monday that the Trump Administration (Trump, RFK Jr., HHS, and/or FDA) are planning to “ban” COVID vaccines in the coming months, Invivyd saw a flood of interest, trading up 84% on Tuesday.

While a full COVID vaccine ban seems unlikely to occur without a clear alternative available for at-risk patients, it is worth pointing out that the CDC and FDA effectively already set the COVID vaccine phaseout in motion back in May (discussed below).

And while these developments may accelerate the recognition of Invivyd’s value, regardless of the Trump Admin’s actions, we have said since beginning coverage of Invivyd that we feel VYD2311 (and other COVID mAbs) will inevitably supplant COVID vaccines as the gold standard for COVID prophylaxis due to superior efficacy, better safety, lack of COVID spike protein introduction into the body, lack of an immune-activating effect, and vaccine-like IM administration.

The hype surrounding Invivyd over the past few days was also fueled by some tweets from widely-followed BioX account Sheep of Wall Street, whose firm (unclear which firm he works for) was one of five key investors that reportedly accounted for 80% of Invivyd’s recent financing. This was the first time that Sheep of Wall Street had ever tweeted about Invivyd. The famous/infamous Adam Feuerstein also engaged on the thread, showing interest in the vaccine ban story and its potential implications for Invivyd.

Irrespective of this week’s news, it has been nice to see Invivyd emerge from August with a clear path and order of operations in front of it after experiencing a difficult few quarters trying to sell a Pemgarda product that management has always readily acknowledged as flawed and cumbersome into a narrow immunocompromised patient market under a technically temporary Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) designation. August’s events give a clear line of sight to the approval of the company’s real potential winner, VYD2311, which could report results from its pivotal trial in 2Q26 if it can conduct the trial efficiently.

We also point out that the FDA’s alignment on a single 12-week trial for VYD2311 suggests a favorable attitude from regulators, especially when compared to the 6 month follow-up requirement that the FDA is newly requiring in COVID vaccine trials as of May. And while it may be a reach, it would seem logical that the smartest and least divisive way for the FDA to achieve its clear goal of eliminating COVID vaccines would be to advance a demonstrably safer, more efficacious, non-vaccine alternative, essentially giving them cover to conduct the rumored vaccine ban.

Speculatively, it also seems plausible that VYD2311 would be a candidate for one of the first Commissioner's National Priority Vouchers (CNPV), an opinion that has also been expressed by Sheep of Wall Street, though the consistently waning prevalence of COVID over the past few years makes this seem less likely.

While this moment of relevance for Invivyd may fade similar to prior stock price spikes, given that the company now has clear simple line of sight for the approval of its highly potent, long half-life VYD2311 antibody, and the capital to support the pivotal trial, it seems likely that some of this attention will convert into new shareholders that are convinced of VYD2311’s significant commercial potential.

We have not added to our position this week, but will consider doing so in the coming weeks/months, especially if the stock drifts back down as hype fades and Invivyd gets into the meat of actually executing its registrational trial.

In our view, if COVID does indeed remain a consistent flu-like seasonal threat (which could be debated given declining trends each of the last two years but seems likely), Invivyd is a clear winner as the only real player in a post-vaccine COVID prophylaxis space, and should trade much higher than the current sub-$200 million valuation in 2026.

Trump Admin Ban

The idea that the Trump Admin may ban COVID vaccines originates from a Newsweek article reporting that a close affiliate of RFK Jr., Dr. Aseem Malhotra, British cardiologist and well-known vaccine critic/skeptic, stated that he believes the initiative to remove COVID vaccines from the market will begin in the next few months, aided by the support of RFK Jr. and “influential members” of Trump’s own family.

Dr. Malhotra discussing the potential dangers of the mRNA COVID vaccines with Megyn Kelly

While it seems unlikely that the COVID vaccines would get completely removed from the market in one fell swoop (mainly because it would cause massive backlash), the measures that have already been taken bythe CDC and FDA lay a clear groundwork for the phasing out of COVID vaccines.

Some of the key of the anti-COVID vaccine measures under the Trump Administration:

  • In May, the CDC removed COVID vaccines from the recommended immunization schedule for healthy children, healthy pregnant women, and healthy adults under the age of 65, i.e. most people. Health insurers, Medicare, and Medicaid usually follow these recommendations when making coverage decisions, meaning the vaccines may no longer be covered for a majority of the population.

  • Also in May, the FDA announced that it would only be considering routine COVID vaccine approvals for seniors (65+) and those with underlying immune complications, meaning variant-updated vaccines would continue to be approved for these at-risk patient populations based on immunogenicity data (simple antibody data), while companies will have to run full-scale randomized, placebo-controlled studies to (re)gain approval in healthy patients.

  • In June, the FDA required Moderna and BioNTech to add warnings to the labeling of COVID vaccines for risk of myocarditis and pericarditis.

  • Also in June, the HHS dismissed/fired all 17 members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP).

  • In August, the HHS announced the winding down of 22 mRNA vaccine development programs at BARDA totalling $500 million in August.

These actions, particularly the changes to the CDC’s recommended immunization schedule and the FDA’s policy regarding COVID vaccine approvals, make it unattractive for COVID vaccine developers to continue pursuing variant-updated vaccines for use in healthy populations, especiallygiven the lower perceived threat of COVID, diminished public interest/trust of COVID vaccines, and the consequently decreased vaccination rates of the past couple years.

Effectively, this regulatory backdrop already began the phaseout of COVID vaccines for most patients:

  • As it stands, there will be no updated COVID vaccine approved for the upcoming fall/winter 2025 vaccination season, and neither Moderna nor Pfizer/BioNTech has guided that they will run trials to pursue (re)approval in healthy patients.

So whether or not the Trump Admin will institute a full, sweeping ban of COVID vaccines is largely irrelevant (from Invivyd’s perspective). The direction of travel is clear—vaccines as a COVID prophylaxis are largely going away, and while we believe VYD2311 and other anti-COVID antibodies would have prevailed as a superior modality anyways, it certainly helps and accelerates their case.

Still, we would say it is unlikely to see a full ban given the complete void that would be left for older/immunocompromised patients, and the backlash that would come as a result, unless the FDA had an alternative COVID preventative that it could offer people.

Interestingly, it really seems that the best course of action for an admin/HHS/FDA that wants to reduce the use of COVID vaccines would be to support the development and promote the benefits of a more effective, less harmful vaccine alternative like Invivyd’s VYD2311.

The Invivyd Story

A brief overview of the Invivyd value proposition:

  1. Anti-COVID monoclonal antibody (mAb) therapies, which provide potent anti-viral defense against COVID without introducing spike proteins/antigens and eliciting the immune response inherent to vaccines, provide superior anti-viral protection and improved safety versus vaccines (as evidenced in the company’s CANOPY trial).

2. Anti-COVID mAbs (developed by Regeneron, Eli Lilly, AstraZeneca) were used in the early days of the pandemic as treatment (not prophylaxis) to reduce viral load and prevent severe disease. Anti-COVID mAbs then largely faded away as vaccines became the public health focus and rapidly mutating variants rendered prior mAbs obsolete.

3. Invivyd, originally founded as Adagio Therapeutics in 2020, developed an anti-COVID antibody called adintrevimab that became ineffective after the Omicron variant. The company then developed Pemgarda (pemivibart), which was awarded Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) in March 2024 for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) in certain immunocompromised patients aged 12 and older.

4. Interestingly, pemivibart has remained active against every variant that has emerged post-Omicron (for which it has been tested, at least). In contrast, AstraZeneca’s sipavibart, which was approved for PrEP in Europe in January 2025 and is one of the only antibodies that has been developed post-Omicron, was found to be ineffective against dominant variants starting in 2024.

5. During the same time, aware of the drawbacks of pemivibart (e.g. IV administration due to lower potency, medium-length half-life), Invivyd developed VYD2311, which has shown 17x higher potency than pemivibart and a 70+ day half-life (versus ~45 days). These improvements enable vaccine-like intramuscular (IM) administration, but should also result in a potentially even greater viral neutralization advantage over vaccines than pemivibart.

6. Invivyd recently announced that it reached alignment with the FDA on a single 12-week Phase 2/3 trial to support potential approval in patients 12 years and older.

The agreement by the FDA to a single, relatively short 12-week trial for approval seems to suggest that the FDA is relatively enthusiastic about VYD2311 and appears to be giving Invivyd credit for the large trials that it ran with VYD2311 predecessor antibodies adintrevimab and pemivibart.

We note that this 12-week trial contrasts sharply with the 6 months of follow-up that the FDA guided it would require for trials of COVID vaccines in healthy populations.

We also note that Invivyd’s proven ability to develop an effective and impressively durable COVID antibody in pemivibart, one that outperforms and outlasts AstraZeneca’s, seems to suggest that its antibody development platform, the specific details of which the company keeps close to the vest, may indeed have some sort of fundamental edge.

While pemivibart and VYD2311 could lose neutralizing capacity to new COVID variants at any time, this potential edge in antibody discovery/development could bode well not only for the longevity or VYD2311 once it potentially reaches the market, but also for Invivyd’s discovery-stage ambitions in RSV and measles.

Timeline: What’s Next

The company has not yet revealed details such as the size or expected start date of VYD2311’s pivotal trial. Assuming Invivyd is going to be able to turn things around quickly as they have in the past (it enrolled the 750-patient CANOPY trial in ~8 weeks in 2023), the trial will be enrolling just in time for the fall/winter “vaccination” season.

Assuming the trial is around 500 patients, the company could start enrolling in October, complete enrollment by December/January, and have 12-week data by March/April. We hope to follow up with management to get a better sense of how quickly it can ramp up.

While normally the 10-month review process would put a potential approval at the turn of 2027 at the earliest, the FDA’s agreement to a relatively short 12-week trial and the situation surrounding COVID vaccines has led myself and others like Sheep of Wall Street to believe that Invivyd could be awarded an expediting designation by the FDA, perhaps even the new Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher (CNPV) program, which includes a two-month review period. Sheep even goes as far as to say that he thinks it is “likely”:

We highly doubt that Sheep would say this if he had any actual knowledge that the FDA has made an indication to the company that they are considering them for the CNPV, but as we mentioned above, the FDA agreement on a single 12-week trial definitely appears favorable, and VYD2311 certainly fits the FDA’s stated priorities for the CNPV program.

To be clear, it is not at all evident that the FDA is taking a particular interest in Invivyd. Even without CNPV (the obvious base case), the FDA should be incentivized to provide Invivyd with a timely review process.

Financial Position

As mentioned, Invivyd just raised $57 million through a combination of equity and pre-funded warrants (both at $0.52/share), which adds to the $35 million on the balance sheet as of 6/30/25. It also has a $30 million credit facility with SVB that is subject to minimum revenue milestones that have not yet been met.

Along with RA Capital and Janus Henderson, Sheep of Wall Street shared that his firm was one of the 5 firms that contributed 80% of the deal.

So the company should have about $80 million on the balance sheet, with an additional $30 million available if it happens to increase Pemgarda sales this fall/winter (probably not likely), and is burning ~$15 million per quarter. If we guess that the trial is probably going to cost at least $25 million (a very rough guess given we don’t even know the size) and will run primarily in 4Q25 and 1Q26, the company could be reporting data and submitting to the FDA in the 2Q26 with at least a couple more quarters of cash runway. Without an expedited review period and if Pemgarda sales stay flat, the company could be running low on cash in the 2H26, at which point we would hope that the COVID mAb narrative has caught more attention and the stock is trading at a higher valuation.

Risks

Declining Covid Prevalence

Covid cases and hospitalizations undoubtedly appear to have declined pretty significantly in each of the last few years. While immunocompromised and other at-risk patients will likely continue to pursue protection, this trend has also coincided with a lower perceived threat in the general public (and physicians), which along with general vaccine distrust has led to dramatically lower COVID vaccination rates year over year, which could also apply to VYD2311 if approved.

That said, it would only take one “bad year”, similar to what occurred this past year with the flu, to substantially reset this public perception.

Confusion with Vaccines

If VYD2311 were approved, some patients may not understand the important differences that differentiate it from a vaccine, and in some cases may avoid prophylaxis based on a fear of vaccines.

Strain Mutation Risk

At any time, circulating COVID strain could mutate such that either pemivibart or VYD2311 are no longer effective against it.

Invivyd’s Historical and Management Struggles

Invivyd has had a bumpy road over the years, going from high-flying stock to in need of capital. The company has seen many executives, including multiple CEOs, come and go. The company also currently does not have a CEO and is primarily run by Chairman Marc Elia, who is capable, but nonetheless not the CEO.

Potential Association with Trump

This may be going out a bit far on a limb, but if VYD2311 does end up getting either CNPV or otherwise being given special FDA attention, many opponents of the Trump Administration, including potential patients, may be biased against VYD2311 as a treatment.

Competition

While there don’t appear to be any clear mAb competitors to VYD2311 pursuing approval in the US at the moment, COVID antibody therapies can be developed by large pharma companies in relatively short order, though their efficacy and durability across viral mutations will be an open question.

Conclusion

Like we have said since long before Monday’s news that the Trump Administration may ban the COVID vaccines, in our minds, VYD2311 (and other COVID mAbs that may follow)—with its superior safety, efficacy, and vaccine-like administration—is a no-brainer to eventually fully supplant vaccines as the clear gold standard for COVID prophylaxis.

While there is some risk that COVID prevalence continues to trend down, the consensus among virologists/scientists appears to be that COVID will remain a persistent seasonal threat, particularly to at-risk patients. VYD2311 is currently the only real candidate positioned to capitalize on a post-vaccine COVID prophylactic landscape, and Invivyd should benefit from the gradual realization of this fact over the coming quarters.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Do Brown-Forman's Q1 Earnings Justify Re-Entering The Stock?
data.description:
Introduction

Shares of Brown-Forman Corporation (NYSE:BF.A, NYSE:BF.B), the company behind Jack Daniel's whiskey, have had a wild ride so far in 2025 and, more generally, have been shunned by the market for several years now.

I have reported on Brown-Forman shares several times here on Seeking Alpha and have always found them too expensive, especially given the company's weak growth. However, in my last article, published in February after BF.A/BF.B shares declined to the low $30 range, I explained why I ultimately thought it made sense to assume a cautious bullish stance. Given the continuing high level of uncertainty, however, I advocated an options-based strategy. I successfully traded BF.A put options over the last six months and was not assigned any shares (although I would not have minded given the strike prices of as little as $25). I bought back my last contracts during the rally ahead of the release of fiscal 2026 first-quarter results, which were published yesterday.

In contrast to the results for the fourth quarter and full year 2025, it appears that the market did not like Brown-Forman's start to the new fiscal year. Shares fell by around 5%, dropping below $30 again, which raises the question of whether I should re-enter the stock and, if so, directly via share ownership or instead once again via put options.

In this article, I will, therefore, provide an overview of Brown-Forman's latest earnings report, but given that I last covered the company in February, I will also give my assessment of the Jack Daniel's manufacturer's fiscal 2025, which ended in July.

Brown-Forman Q1 FY2026 Earnings Review And My Longer-Term View

In the first quarter of fiscal 2026, Brown-Forman generated net sales of $924 million, which was slightly better than expected but still represents a 3% decline compared to the previous year (Figure 1). However, organic net sales rose 1% year-over-year, although this should not be interpreted as a sign of recovery, as the company expects organic sales to decline in the low single digits for the full year.

Operating earnings declined 7% and diluted earnings per share declined 13% year-over-year, slightly below consensus. However, it is important to note that the result was not influenced by one-off effects and Brown-Forman refrained from reporting adjusted earnings.

The ongoing weakness in earnings (the reasons for which I explained in detail in my previous coverage) is naturally disappointing, but I was nonetheless pleased to see that gross margin was maintained at almost 60% and even recovered by 33 basis points compared to the previous year (Figure 2). Given the easy comparison and the seasonality of the business, the sequential gross margin expansion of 236 basis points should not, of course, be overrated.

Brown-Forman is clearly maintaining good cost control despite the current difficult environment. In addition to the resilient gross margin, it is worth noting that selling, general, and administrative expenses for the quarter were $177 million, which is in line with the five-year average but represents a 6% decrease year-over-year. Advertising expenses declined 5% year-over-year -which isn't necessarily positive - but as can be seen in Figure 3, they remain in line with historical trends, even relatively speaking as a percentage of net sales. Given the current unfavorable demand trend and product innovation, I would like to see an increase in advertising spending, even if it comes at additional expense of profits.

Taken together, the (modest) gross margin expansion and the decline in selling, general, and administrative expenses and advertising spending should have led to an increase in operating margin, but instead there was a decline of 1.4 percentage points. The main reasons for this were restructuring costs (which were not excluded from reported income) and lower other income (partly due to currency derivatives).

Finally, the reason for the double-digit decline in earnings per share (compared to a 7% decline in operating income) was a postretirement expense of $19 million, which was slightly offset by lower interest expenses and lower income taxes. The number of shares used to calculate earnings per share remained virtually unchanged from the previous year, so dilution from stock options did not play a role.

Given the difficult demand environment and, most recently, also the tariffs situation, it is clear that investing in Brown-Forman shares requires patience. This is also why I continue to approach the investment via put options. I think it says a lot that current EPS expectations for fiscal 2026 are almost identical to Brown-Forman's earnings for fiscal 2018 (Figure 4), and it doesn't take much imagination to see that the stock is likely to remain what is often referred to as "dead money" for the foreseeable future.

For those who follow Brown-Forman and its peers, it likely comes as no surprise that inventories remain extremely high, both in absolute terms and as a percentage of trailing twelve months net sales (Figure 5). However, given the specifics of Brown-Forman's business, I doubt that the company will have to take significant inventory-related impairment charges going forward.

That said, finished goods inventory has grown at an above-average rate over the last two quarters, up 8% from the fourth quarter and 20% from the third quarter of fiscal 2025, which is obviously putting pressure on profits and cash flows. However, management is committed to reduce the surplus in due course. At the same time, while this increase is certainly noteworthy, the general trend of an increase in finished goods from the third quarter onwards is normal. After all, the third quarter includes the holiday season.

Given the ongoing operational challenges and high inventory levels (which impact cash flow through tied-up working capital), it is entirely justified to take a fresh look at Brown-Forman's debt and free cash flow. This also applies with regard to the dividend (current starting yield 3.1%), which is likely to be the only significant return factor over the near term.

From a cash flow perspective, fiscal 2026 started off rather well for the company, with operating cash flow of $160 million, driven by a decline in accounts receivable and lower inventory additions than in the same period last year. Similarly, payables and accruals increased less than over the first quarter of fiscal 2025. That said, in light of the dynamics throughout the year and Brown-Forman's seasonality, I wouldn't overemphasize the good cash inflow from operations in Q1.

Hence, and while these observations leave a positive impression, it is worth taking a look at Brown-Forman's performance in fiscal 2025. Remember that after the post-pandemic recovery, free cash flow declined from the typical $600 million to $800 million per year to only around $400 million in fiscal 2023. I was glad to see that free cash flow for fiscal 2025 came in at $403 million, after adjusting for stock-based compensation (Figure 6). Note that I have refrained from smoothing out the impact of working capital, as it is currently uncertain how long it will take Brown-Forman to bring its inventories back to normal levels.

Brown-Forman currently pays out its entire free cash flow in dividends, and even its average payout ratio over the last four years is 100% in terms of free cash flow. Although the company generally pays out most or all of its free cash flow - as demonstrated for example by the special dividend in fiscal 2022 - I would prefer to see a slightly more conservative approach in light of the current environment. At the same time, I appreciate management's commitment to maintaining (and even increasing) the dividend despite the current headwinds.

From a leverage perspective, I would argue that this behavior is not fully justified. Net debt remains comparatively high at $2.3 billion (Figure 7), representing almost six times current free cash flow. This leaves little or no room for acquisitions, while at the same time the company is currently unable to repay any debt due to its 100% payout ratio. However, given that the conventionally calculated leverage ratio is only about 2x net debt to EBITDA for fiscal 2025, I would not overemphasize the issue from a creditor's perspective. Also, Brown-Forman still has an A1 credit rating (Moody's), but the rating agency has – quite understandably – changed the outlook to negative back in late 2024 due to the elevated leverage.

Personally, I consider the dividend reasonably safe for now, but long-term investors need to factor in the risk of a rebasement if the demand environment does not improve significantly in the coming years and interest rates do not decline as expected. Of course, management could buy more time by monetizing assets (as for example in fiscal 2025, $350 million), but that is clearly not something I want to see on a regular basis, especially in a difficult market environment. I would much rather prefer to see such transactions during boom phases.

Conclusion: Why I Maintain My Options-Based Strategy With Brown-Forman Stock

Brown-Forman Corporation continues to operate in a challenging demand environment, but I would argue that the results for the first quarter of fiscal 2026 are as good as they could be given the circumstances. I was particularly pleased to see gross margin remain stable, which is a key pillar of my investment thesis for Brown-Forman. As shown on slide 7 of the earnings presentation, gross margin remained stable despite a slight currency headwind, mainly thanks to portfolio optimizations. It is important to note that pricing and mix did not contribute to gross margin expansion in the first quarter, underscoring the strength of the portfolio in light of organic top-line performance.

Also noteworthy is the continued good cost control in sales, general, and administrative expenses and advertising expenditures, although I would like to see a temporary focus on above-average advertising specifically targeting ready-to-drink products and newly introduced flavored high-alcohol products (e.g., Jack Daniel's Blackberry and other flavors).

While sales in the domestic market and in developed international markets were disappointing as expected (down 2% and 9% year-over-year organically, respectively), the trend in emerging markets was promising (up 25% year-over-year organically). And although this segment still only makes a small contribution to Brown-Forman's top-line, I am nevertheless very pleased that this traditionally U.S.-focused company is increasingly turning its attention to emerging markets, which I believe (alongside product innovations) are the most important growth driver going forward.

Given the difficult demand environment and, most recently, the tariffs situation, it is rather obvious that investing in Brown-Forman shares requires patience. Free cash flow is likely to remain subdued also in fiscal 2026, and it will most certainly take years for the company to return to sustainable free cash flow of well over $600 million annually. This implies a forward free cash flow yield of 4.4%, which I consider reasonable for rating BF.A/BF.B shares a cautious buy. The forward P/E ratio of slightly below 18 points in the same direction, although Brown-Forman stock is certainly not trading at a significant discount to fair value (see my previous article) and its longer-term earnings performance is definitely disappointing.

For these reasons, I believe Brown-Forman stock could remain "dead money" for the foreseeable future (aside from the 3% dividend yield). Therefore, I will continue to approach this investment through put options. I plan to sell put options expiring on October 17 or December 19 with strike prices of $25 and $27.50, as I am not overly optimistic about Brown-Forman's share price in the short term. If I am assigned the shares, I would not mind at the implied valuation, but at the same time, I can see myself continuing to trade options and earning the reasonable returns associated with them. For example, the put expiring on December 19 with a strike of $27.50 was trading at $1.60 shortly before yesterday's close, which corresponds to an implied annualized return of 20%.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: CLM: Unique High-Yield Income Opportunity, But Only For Active Investors
data.description:
Overview

I'd argue that Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (NYSE:CLM) is one of the most unique income funds available to investors today. While the high yield is what may attract most people initially, there is another benefit to this fund that can compound your wealth and total returns rapidly over time. When I previously covered this fund, I issued a buy rating and I briefly talked about this benefit. However, I've seen lots of confusion and misunderstanding about how CLM operates so I wanted to revisit this fund to lay out the exact blueprint on how to properly utilize it.

While the long term performance chart of CLM may look scary, there's a perfectly logical reason why CLM's share price has a long history of declines. As we can see below, CLM's share price has declined by 52.3% over the last ten years. However, the total return jumps up to 232.5% when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders. At the time of this writing, the dividend now sits at 18% and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. However, the chart below doesn't take the DRIP benefit into account here, which I will discuss later on.

Unlike most other high-yield funds, CLM isn't a simple buy-and-hold investment. I believe there are certain rules and structures you must follow in order to get the most out of your position. For instance, CLM offers a high dividend yield, but I'd argue that you shouldn't actually use any of the dividends received; In order to properly utilize the fund, you absolutely must reinvest distribution back down at the fund's NAV, which is how the real compounding magic happens. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying holdings of the fund.

Fund Holdings

What is unique about CLM is that the fund issues a large distribution while still holding traditional equities. The fund doesn't utilize any sort of fancy synthetic option writing strategies to generate the earnings it needs to fuel the distributions. Instead, CLM allocates a smaller portion of its total assets to different income-focused closed end funds to help boost the income that it generates. According to the latest semi-annual report for 2025, CLM allocates approximately 6.6% of assets to these income-focused assets, including the following:

  • Convertible Security Funds
  • Energy MLP Funds
  • Global Funds
  • Diversified Equity Funds
  • Income & Preferred Stock
  • Option Strategy Funds
  • Real Estate Focused Funds
  • Utilities Focused Funds

However, common stocks make up the overwhelming majority of the fund, so it makes sense to shift our focus there. Looking at the equities breakdown by sector, we get a well-rounded allocation that looks parallel to the S&P 500 (SPX). For instance, Technology holdings account for 30.45% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Consumer Cyclical companies, which account for 13.33% and 11.90% of assets respectively. Therefore, investors should anticipate CLM to follow the daily price movement of the S&P 500.

Looking at the top ten holdings below, we can see that CLM provides exposure to many of the highest quality companies in the world. For instance, Apple (AAPL) is the largest position with a weight of 7.42%. This is followed by other large-cap companies such as Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Tesla (TSLA) just to name a few. Since CLM also implements a generous distribution rate alongside these holdings, the fund has the ability to outperform traditional indexes.

Just to demonstrate this concept, I pulled in a performance chart of CLM over the last twelve months. As we can see below CLM's total return significantly outpaces the total return of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) over the last year. CLM has a total return of 28.17%, while SPY's total return is only 16.58%. When the fund is utilized correctly, I believe that investors can replicate this outperformance every single year.

The Blueprint

Since CLM operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying NAV. The main differentiating benefit of CLM is that all distributions received can be DRIP (dividend reinvestment plan) back down at the fund's NAV. As far as I know, CLM and Cornerstone Total Return Fund (CRF) are the only funds that offer this sort of benefit to investors. So if CLM is priced at $7.85 per share, but the NAV is worth $6.80 per share, all distributions will be automatically reinvested back down at the NAV. The fund's DRIP report lays out details about the process that I suggest reading through.

CLM's official net asset value table updates every week, but it's a quick way to reference how large of a premium CLM's price is trading compared to its NAV. As we can see below, the last updated table is from August 22nd, and states that the fund trades at a premium to NAV of 18.97%.

Step 1: Keep An Eye Out For Rights Offerings

When the fund's premium to NAV becomes too large, management implements a rights offering. When the fund trades at a large premium to NAV, management can capture that premium and turn it into cash by issuing new shares. A rights offering essentially allows investor to buy new shares at a closer price to its actual NAV. By issuing new shares and collecting cash, CLM can continue to grow its base assets under management year after year. These rights offerings are what causes CLM's share price to decline over the long-term.

The most recent rights offering was completed in May of 2025, so I don't anticipate another one taking place for at least another 12 months. The details of the rights offering were as follows:

Subscription Price

  1. 112% of NAV per share
  2. 80% of the market price per share

At the close of the offering, the formula resulted in a final price of $7.30 per share. These formulas are in place to ensure that CLM isn't selling shares for too cheap, but also to protect existing shareholders. To help conceptualize this, let's use the example below -

Suppose CLM has a NAV of $6.00 and the market price of $7.85 per share.

  • 112% of the NAV = $6.72 per share
  • 80% of the market price = $6.28 per share

The 112% is the higher of the two, so this will be the subscription price for the rights offering.

Step 2: Learn The Premium Thresholds

The most important step to utilizing CLM is to understand the premium to NAV thresholds. Rights offerings are typically announced when the premiums are very large. To help visualize this, I added red arrows to represent the periods when a rights offering was announced for CLM. Each time a rights offering was announced, the share price tends to see a large decline. So for me personally, I choose to sell out of my position as soon as I see a Form N-2 filed, which is an indication that a rights offering will be taking place.

For instance, the last N-2 filing was completed in February of 2025. Based on the historical performance, CLM sees a large decline right after the N-2 filing is revealed. For instance, I pulled in a chart of CLM's price on a YTD basis below. We can see how CLM saw a large decline near the end of February.

So the general rule of thumb that I follow is to only accumulate shares when CLM's premium to NAV is below 20%. Once the premium sits above 20% to its NAV, I stop accumulating shares and simply reinvest dividends. Once an N-2 filing is revealed, I immediately sell out of my position and then re-enter once the rights offering is completed.

Step 3: You Must Reinvest Distributions

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1224 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 18%. However, I believe that investors shouldn't actually withdraw or spend these distributions if they don't need to. Instead, I think it's more beneficial to reinvest distributions every single month. Since CLM now trades at a premium to NAV of around 19%, this means that all dividends being reinvested are receiving an instant 19% capital gain on all shares. So not only are you getting a high rate of income, but you are also compounding shares and expanding the equity of your position rapidly.

While I cannot confirm that all brokerages participate in this process, I have had no issues with Fidelity. Just to demonstrate, last month I received a distribution and it was automatically reinvested back down at the NAV. At the time, the NAV was around $6.85 per share, while the share price hovered above $8.00 per share at the time.

So in this sense, I don't think that CLM should be viewed as a traditional income position. Since rights offerings essentially dilute the fund over time, the share price and distribution payouts will consistently decline over time. That's why the dividend history below indicates that the payouts have declined over time. The most efficient way to offset this is to simply reinvest distributions over time and take advantage of the DRIP benefit.

Risks

CLM requires investors to actively manage their position and keep up with the filings. If you miss an N-2 filing and the share price collapses on you may see your capital erode over time. Additionally, I've been made aware by readers that some brokerages simply don't support the DRIP benefit. If your brokerage doesn't support reinvesting distributions back down at the NAV, you are missing a critical piece of the formula and I don't believe it's worth holding a position in the fund without it.

CLM will not be a good fit for investors that simply want to buy-and-hold through the next decade. Assuming several more rights offerings in the future, both the share price and payout amounts will continue their downward trends. If you aren't taking advantage of the DRIP benefit and simply holding, you are likely to underperform more traditional index tracking ETFs as well. As we can see below, CLM underperformed SPY over the last decade.

Lastly, the distributions received may cause tax consequences for investors over the long term. Looking at the semi-annual report for 2025 below, we can see that distributions were mostly fueled by the combination of net investment income, net realized gains, and return of capital. Net investment income is typically classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the highest tax burden. Net realized gains can technically be more tax-efficient if they are long-term capital gains but it is not specified in the reporting.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate CLM as a buy at this time due to the price trading at a premium to NAV under the 20% threshold. I plan to continue accumulating shares and reinvesting them back down at the NAV until the next N-2 filing, which I don't anticipate happening over the next year. The fund requires an active management style and I wouldn't suggest it for investors that want a buy-and-hold experience. Since the fund maintains exposure to equities that are representative of the S&P 500, CLM's daily price movements will generally match the indexes. So investors are still able to participate in market rallies with CLM, but will need to sell if the premium becomes too large and a rights offering is announced.
